topic: space heater season — apartment fire calls spike

turns.content: seven space heater fires in ten days. seven.
That's a brutal stretch. Residential space heaters, I'm guessing? Those things are responsible for a huge share of winter fire deaths every year.
all residential. apartments mostly. same story every time. extension cord, combustibles too close, unattended. one lady had hers sitting on top of a cardboard box. a cardboard box.
On a cardboard box. That's unbelievable. Were there any fatalities in those seven calls?

one. elderly man in a second floor unit in Kensington. space heater tipped over onto a pile of clothes while he was sleeping. smoke got him before the fire did. neighbors called it in but by the time we forced entry it was too late.
That's a tough one. Especially when it's preventable. Carbon monoxide and smoke inhalation move fast when someone's asleep — sometimes there's just not enough time even with a quick response.
no smoke detector either. building was supposed to have them per code. landlord will have to answer for that. but the old man is still gone.
Yeah, code violations don't bring anyone back. Is the fire marshal's office going after the landlord on that?

they better be. I sent my report up. that building had issues. listen every February it's the same thing. temperatures drop and people plug in whatever they can find. half these heaters are 20 years old with frayed cords. we do the outreach, we do the PSAs, but people don't listen until something burns.

It's a frustrating cycle. The education campaigns help on the margins but the people most at risk — elderly, low-income, older housing stock — are the hardest to reach. Some cities have had luck with free space heater exchange programs where they swap old units for newer ones with tip-over shutoffs. Has Philly tried anything like that?

we did a pilot a few years ago. ran out of funding after one winter. city council said they'd revisit it. still waiting on that. meanwhile my guys are running three and four calls a shift some nights.
That's exhausting. Three to four calls a shift during a cold snap — your crews are getting ground down. Is the department pushing council to bring the exchange program back?

the commissioner mentioned it at the last meeting. whether it actually moves I don't know. I just worry about my guys burning out. no pun intended. February and March are the worst months. then it calms down. just gotta get through it.
Dark humor keeps you sane in that line of work. Your crews are lucky to have a chief who's tracking the toll it takes on them, not just the call volume. Hang in there through March — spring's coming.
